title: High-Speed Lambda Comparators & PriorityQueues
subtitle: Multi-key ordering, bounded heaps, and the boxing tax on the hot path
goal: Build multi-dimensional comparators that sort millions of records under tight time and memory budgets, exploit stable-sort guarantees instead of fighting them, drive PriorityQueue with custom orderings for streaming top-K, and recognize the contract violations and autoboxing costs that quietly destroy latency in an HFT order book.

blocks.heading: A comparator is a total order, not a predicate
blocks.html:
A Comparator<T> answers one question: given a and b, is a before, equal to, or after b? It returns a negative int, 0, or a positive int — the magnitude is meaningless, only the sign matters. Arrays.sort, Collections.sort, PriorityQueue, TreeMap and the Stream.sorted pipeline all consume this one contract.

In an HFT context you almost never sort on a single field. You sort an order book by price, then time priority, then sequence id — a lexicographic chain across dimensions. The whole tier is about expressing that chain cheaply and correctly.

blocks.caption: comparing / thenComparing — the lexicographic chain
blocks.explain: thenComparing only consults the next key when the previous key reports a tie (returns 0). This short-circuit is the entire point: most pairs differ on price, so ts and seq are rarely evaluated. Build the chain from most-selective key to least-selective for the cheapest average comparison.

blocks.heading: comparingInt / comparingLong / comparingDouble — kill the boxing
blocks.html:
Comparator.comparing(Order::seq) where seq is a long forces the extractor's return value to be boxed into a Long object on every comparison, then unboxed by Long.compareTo. On a sort of n elements that is O(n log n) comparisons, each allocating throwaway wrapper objects — a firehose of garbage that hammers the young-gen collector.

The specialized factories comparingInt, comparingLong, comparingDouble (and their thenComparingInt/Long/Double siblings) take a primitive-returning functional interface (ToIntFunction, etc.) and compare with Integer.compare/Long.compare/Double.compare — zero boxing. On a hot path this is not a micro-optimization; it is the difference between a clean sort and a GC storm.

Do not write comparingInt(o -> (int) o.price()) just to dodge boxing — truncating a double to an int destroys ordering. Use comparingDouble for doubles and reserve comparingInt/Long for genuinely integral keys. And never implement an int comparator as a - b: that overflows for large/negative values (e.g. Integer.MIN_VALUE - 1 wraps positive) and silently corrupts the order. Always Integer.compare(a, b).

blocks.heading: reversed() and mixed ascending/descending keys
blocks.html:
comparator.reversed() flips the entire order. But the common HFT need is mixed: sort price descending (best bid first) but, within equal price, time priority ascending (oldest first). Reversing the whole chain would also reverse the tie-break, which is wrong.

The fix is to reverse only the offending key. Two idioms: pass a reversed key comparator into thenComparing, or for primitives, negate inside a custom lambda. The cleanest is per-key:

blocks.caption: Mixed-direction multi-key sort done right
blocks.explain: comparingDouble(...).reversed() reverses ONLY the price key; the subsequent thenComparingLong still sorts ts ascending. Reversing args (b vs a) inside Long.compare is the boxing-free hand-rolled equivalent of a descending primitive key — useful when you want one allocation-free lambda for the whole chain.

blocks.heading: Stable sort: a guarantee you can build on
blocks.html:
Java's object sorts (Arrays.sort(Object[]), Collections.sort, List.sort, Stream.sorted) use TimSort, which is stable: elements the comparator calls equal keep their original relative order. The primitive sorts (Arrays.sort(int[])) use a dual-pivot quicksort and are not stable — but stability is meaningless for bare primitives anyway.

Stability lets you sort in passes: sort by the weakest key first, then re-sort by a stronger key; equal-on-the-strong-key elements retain the weak-key order for free. In practice you should still build one combined comparator (one pass, fewer cache misses), but the guarantee is what makes a thenComparing chain behave predictably and what lets a streaming pipeline preserve arrival order among ties.

blocks.heading: PriorityQueue: a binary heap, not a sorted list
blocks.html:
PriorityQueue<T> is a binary min-heap over an array. peek() is O(1) and returns the smallest element per the comparator; offer() and poll() are O(log n) sift operations. Pass a comparator to control "smallest": new PriorityQueue<>(cmp). With no comparator it uses natural ordering and requires elements to be Comparable.

Crucial gotchas: iteration order is NOT sorted — only poll emits elements in priority order; iterating the queue gives heap-array order. And the head is the minimum. For a max-priority queue (best bid on top), invert the comparator: cmp.reversed() or Comparator.reverseOrder().

blocks.caption: Top-K with a bounded min-heap: O(n log k), not O(n log n)
blocks.explain: Memory is O(k), not O(n): you never hold the whole stream. Each element costs at most one O(log k) offer/poll, so total work is O(n log k). For k far smaller than n this crushes the naive 'sort all then take k' (O(n log n) time and O(n) space) — the canonical pattern for 'top 100 orders out of a 1e6 feed'.

blocks.html: Bounded-heap top-K is the streaming workhorse, but watch the heap element type. A PriorityQueue<Integer> boxes every int. On a 1e6 feed with a large k that is real garbage; for the absolute hot path people drop to a hand-rolled long[] heap or pack (price, id) into a single long key to stay primitive. Know the trade-off before you reach for it.

blocks.heading: The contract: transitivity, or IllegalArgumentException
blocks.html:
A comparator must define a total order: it must be (1) antisymmetric — sgn(compare(a,b)) == -sgn(compare(b,a)); (2) transitive — if a < b and b < c then a < c; and (3) consistent with equality on ties. If your comparator violates this — the classic bug is subtraction overflow, or a hand-rolled comparator with inconsistent branches — TimSort may detect the inconsistency and throw:

java.lang.IllegalArgumentException: Comparison method violates its general contract!

This is not always thrown — TimSort only catches it on certain merge patterns, so a broken comparator can pass small inputs and blow up in production on a large one. Treat the exception as proof your ordering logic is wrong, never as a flaky sort.

blocks.question: You sort 5,000,000 orders by a long `seq` field. Which extractor avoids autoboxing on every comparison?
blocks.explain: comparingLong takes a ToLongFunction (primitive long) and compares with Long.compare — no Long objects are created. comparing(Order::seq) boxes the long to a Long on every one of the ~n log n comparisons, generating massive young-gen garbage.

blocks.question: What is the error in `Comparator<Integer> c = (a, b) -> a - b;` used to sort arbitrary ints?
options: Nothing — subtraction is the idiomatic int comparator.
It boxes, which is slow but correct.
a - b can overflow int (e.g. large positive minus large negative), flipping the sign and violating transitivity — use Integer.compare(a, b).
It sorts descending instead of ascending.
blocks.explain: For a = Integer.MAX_VALUE, b = -1, a - b overflows to a negative number, so the comparator claims MAX_VALUE < -1. This breaks the total-order contract and can trigger 'Comparison method violates its general contract!'. Always use Integer.compare.

blocks.question: You need a max-priority queue (largest element at the head) of Orders by price. Which construction is correct?
options: A PriorityQueue is always max-heap by default
blocks.explain: PriorityQueue is a MIN-heap relative to its comparator: the head is the comparator's smallest. Reversing the price comparator makes the largest price compare as 'smallest', so it sits at the head. PriorityQueue has no sort() and is min-oriented by default.

blocks.title: Stable two-key sort by hand
blocks.instructions: Sort the records {("b",2),("a",2),("c",1)} by the int key ascending, then — because the sort is stable — verify ties keep input order. Print the names in sorted order, space-separated, on one line. Use comparingInt to avoid boxing.
blocks.passMsg: Correct — c (key 1) first; among the two key-2 records, b stays before a because TimSort is stable and b came first in the input.
blocks.failMsg: Expected 'c b a'. key 1 sorts first; the two key-2 records keep their INPUT order (b before a) because the object sort is stable.

problems.title: Three-key order-book comparator hot path
problems.instructions:
Sort an order book by a three-level lexicographic rule under a tight budget. Line 1: an integer n (1 ≤ n ≤ 1,000,000). Then n lines, each an order: price ts seq where price is a long (integer ticks), ts is a long (nanos), seq is a long unique id.

Order the book by: price descending (best bid first); ties broken by ts ascending (oldest first); remaining ties broken by seq ascending.

Output the seq of every order, one per line, in sorted order. Use a boxing-free primitive comparator and fast IO — the hidden stress test is 1,000,000 orders inside a 2.5s budget.

tests.name: visible: price desc, ts asc, seq asc
hidden: full three-way tie resolves on seq
hidden: dense ties across all three keys

problems.hint: Swap the argument order inside Long.compare(b[0], a[0]) for the descending key — no .reversed() needed and no boxing. Keep ts and seq ascending with the natural argument order. Build all output in one StringBuilder; per-line System.out.println will blow the 1e6 budget.

problems.title: Streaming top-K with a bounded min-heap
problems.instructions:
From a stream of trade sizes, report the K largest using O(K) memory — you may not hold the whole stream sorted. Line 1: n k (1 ≤ k ≤ n ≤ 1,000,000). Line 2: n space-separated ints (each fits in an int, may be negative).

Maintain a bounded min-heap of size k: the head is the smallest of the current best-K; when a new value exceeds the head, evict the head and insert the new value. Ties (equal values) are fine — duplicates are allowed.

Output the K largest values in descending order, space-separated, on one line. The hidden stress test feeds 1,000,000 values; the bounded heap keeps you at O(n log k).

tests.name: hidden: k equals n returns all sorted desc

problems.hint: A MIN-heap of size k holds the k largest seen: its head is the weakest survivor, so compare new values against heap.peek(). Drain the heap into an array (that gives ascending) and print in reverse for descending. Use StreamTokenizer, not split(), to parse 1e6 ints in time.

problems.title: Mixed-direction sort: best bid, oldest first
problems.instructions:
Sort resting orders the way a matching engine ranks bids. Line 1: n (1 ≤ n ≤ 500,000). Then n lines: name price ts where name is a token with no spaces, price is a long, ts is a long.

Rank by price descending; within equal price by ts ascending (older orders have priority); within equal price and ts, preserve input order (rely on the stable object sort — do not add a tertiary key).

Output each name on its own line in ranked order. The point is mixing one descending and one ascending key on a single comparator while leaning on TimSort's stability for the final tie.

tests.name: visible: price desc, ts asc, stable tie A/C/D
hidden: equal price and ts keep input order

problems.hint: comparingLong(Order::price).reversed() reverses ONLY the price key; the chained thenComparingLong(Order::ts) still sorts ts ascending. Add no third key — TimSort is stable, so equal (price, ts) orders keep their input order automatically.

problems.title: Detect a comparator that violates the contract
problems.instructions:
Before trusting a custom comparator on a 1e6 sort, validate it defines a total order. You are given n distinct integers and a comparator defined by the rule below; check it for the antisymmetry and transitivity failures that trigger "Comparison method violates its general contract!".

The comparator under test is the buggy subtraction comparator (a, b) -> a - b. Line 1: n (1 ≤ n ≤ 1000). Line 2: n space-separated ints (may be near Integer.MIN_VALUE/MAX_VALUE).

Check every ordered pair (a, b): compute cmp = a - b (as an int, allowing overflow) and the correct sign from Integer.compare(a, b). If for any pair signum(cmp) disagrees with the correct sign, the comparator is broken.

Output exactly one line: BROKEN <count> where count is the number of ordered pairs (a, b), a≠b, whose subtraction sign is wrong; or OK if none disagree.

tests.name: hidden: small values never overflow -> OK
hidden: min and max both directions

problems.hint: v[i]-v[j] in int arithmetic overflows when the true difference exceeds 2^31-1; Integer.signum of that wrapped result then disagrees with Integer.compare. Count every ordered pair (both directions) where the signs differ. Distinct values mean Integer.compare is never 0.
